summary:
An angiofibroma extending into one or more paranasal sinuses meets criteria for being categorized as a Stage II lesion based multiple established classifications systems which focus primarily on degree of anatomical spread rather than purely vascular characteristics alone at these stages.
```

Key Clinical Points Summary by Agent1: The critical point here involves understanding how differentiating between Stages I vs II hinges mainly upon whether the growth has invaded any sinus cavities; such infiltration automatically classifies it under Stage II regardless of its size or blood supply status within those confines per widely recognized medical guidelines including Radkowsky et al., Andrew-Fish, UPenn protocols etc..
The main takeaway then becomes recognizing that presence + penetration = definite progression from initial benign appearance towards higher risk/more aggressive behavior warranting closer monitoring / potential intervention depending also on patient symptoms & overall health condition alongside imaging findings confirming exact location/extent of disease propagation beyond nasal cavity walls directly impacting adjacent structures via direct contact/conduit pathways created through bone erosion processes characteristic seen during advanced cases.. ``` 
An angiofibroma invading one or more paranasal sinuses is classified as Stage II due to extensive local tissue invasion, aligning across major diagnostic frameworks despite variations in specific details about additional features considered at later stages.
This indicates significant localized expansion requiring careful management considering both structural damage caused and functional impacts on nearby areas potentially leading to complications necessitating surgical evaluation/intervention where indicated clinically along with regular follow-up care.
```
